vulnerability.title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
vulnerability.description: SSSD should be configured to verify the certificate of the user or group. To set this up ensure that section like certmap/testing.test/rule_name is setup in /etc/sssd/sssd.conf. For example [certmap/testing.test/rule_name] matchrule =<SAN>.*EDIPI@mil maprule = (userCertificate;binary={cert!bin}) domains = testing.test
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Enable Certmap in SSSD

triage.reason: The remediation involves adding a certmap stanza to /etc/sssd/sssd.conf, a straightforward configuration edit. While an incorrect mapping could affect authentication, the change follows standard STIG hardening patterns and does not inherently risk system boot or critical services. Therefore it is considered safe to remediate with proper testing.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential authentication failure if certmap syntax is incorrect; Misconfiguration could prevent users from logging in via certificate

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly adds the required certmap stanza and restarts sssd, which should satisfy the OpenSCAP rule. Minor improvements (backup, validation, idempotency) would make it safer and more robust.
concerns: The plan does not back up the existing sssd.conf before overwriting it.
No syntax validation (e.g., sssctl config-check) is performed before restarting the service, which could cause a service failure if the stanza is malformed.
If the stanza already exists, the plan may create duplicate entries.
suggested_improvements: Create a timestamped backup of /etc/sssd/sssd.conf before modifying it.
Run 'sssctl config-check' or 'sssd --test' after writing the file to verify syntax.
Implement an idempotent check to add the stanza only if it is not already present.
Consider restarting sssd with 'systemctl try-restart sssd' to avoid unnecessary downtime if the service is already active.

qa_result.verdict_reason: Appending a new certmap stanza without backing up the original file or validating the syntax may cause SSSD to fail to start, which would break authentication services (e.g., SSH login) and could lock out users. Duplicate entries could also lead to configuration errors.
side_effects: Potential SSSD service failure
Authentication lockout for users relying on SSSD (e.g., SSH, sudo)
Duplicate configuration entries if stanza already exists

pre_approval.rejection_reason: Appending a new certmap stanza without backing up the original file or validating the syntax may cause SSSD to fail to start, which would break authentication services (e.g., SSH login) and could lock out users. Duplicate entries could also lead to configuration errors.

vulnerability.title: Enable Smartcards in SSSD
vulnerability.description: SSSD should be configured to authenticate access to the system using smart cards. To enable smart cards in SSSD, set pam_cert_auth to True under the [pam] section in /etc/sssd/sssd.conf. For example: [pam] pam_cert_auth = True Add or update "pam_sss.so" line in auth section of "/etc/pam.d/system-auth" file to include "try_cert_auth" or "require_cert_auth" option, like in the following example: /etc/pam.d/system-auth:auth [success=done authinfo_unavail=ignore ignore=ignore default=die] pam_sss.so try_cert_auth Also add or update "pam_sss.so" line in auth section of "/etc/pam.d/smartcard-auth" file to include the "allow_missing_name" option, like in the following example: /etc/pam.d/smartcard-auth:auth sufficient pam_sss.so allow_missing_name
vulnerability.recommendation:
Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q sssd-common; then # sssd configuration files must be created with 600 permissions if they don't exist # otherwise the sssd module fails to start OLD_UMASK=$(umask) umask u=rw,go= found=false # set value in all files if they contain section or key for f in $(echo -n "/etc/sssd/sssd.conf /etc/sssd/conf.d/*.conf"); do if [ ! -e "$f" ]; then continue fi # find key in section and change value if grep -qzosP "[[:space:]]*\[pam\]([^
\[]*
+)+?[[:space:]]*pam_cert_auth" "$f"; then if ! grep -qPz "pam_cert_auth=True" "$f"; then sed -i "s/pam_cert_auth[^(
)]*/pam_cert_auth=True/" "$f" fi found=true # find section and add key = value to it elif grep -qs "[[:space:]]*\[pam\]" "$f"; then sed -i "/[[:space:]]*\[pam\]/a pam_cert_au

triage.reason: The remediation only adds or updates PAM and SSSD configuration entries to enable smart‑card authentication without removing existing password mechanisms. This is a standard STIG‑style auth change and is unlikely to cause immediate lockout or service failure if applied correctly.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential authentication lockout if PAM syntax is incorrect; SSSD service may need to be restarted, which could temporarily affect domain lookups

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly adds pam_cert_auth = True to SSSD and attempts to add try_cert_auth to PAM, but it does not add the required allow_missing_name option to /etc/pam.d/smartcard-auth and it only edits /etc/sssd/sssd.conf, ignoring possible *.conf files in /etc/sssd/conf.d/. Therefore the vulnerability would likely remain flagged.
concerns: Missing allow_missing_name option in /etc/pam.d/smartcard-auth as required by the STIG.
Only /etc/sssd/sssd.conf is modified; additional conf.d/*.conf files are ignored.
Sed commands may unintentionally modify commented lines or create duplicate entries.
No backup of configuration files before in‑place editing.
No service restart or verification step; changes may not take effect until SSSD is reloaded.
suggested_improvements: Add a step to edit any *.conf files under /etc/sssd/conf.d/ that contain a [pam] section, applying the same pam_cert_auth = True change.
Update the smartcard-auth PAM file to include the allow_missing_name option, e.g., `sed -i '/pam_sss.so/ { /allow_missing_name/! s/$/ allow_missing_name/ }' /etc/pam.d/smartcard-auth`.
Create backups (`cp -a file file.bak`) before applying sed modifications.
After configuration changes, restart or reload the sssd service (systemctl restart sssd) and optionally run pam-auth-update.
Consider verifying SELinux context of the edited files (restorecon) to avoid access issues.

pre_approval.rejection_reason: The plan correctly adds pam_cert_auth = True to SSSD and attempts to add try_cert_auth to PAM, but it does not add the required allow_missing_name option to /etc/pam.d/smartcard-auth and it only edits /etc/sssd/sssd.conf, ignoring possible *.conf files in /etc/sssd/conf.d/. Therefore the vulnerability would likely remain flagged.

vulnerability.title: Enable auditd Service
vulnerability.description: The auditd service is an essential userspace component of the Linux Auditing System, as it is responsible for writing audit records to disk. The auditd service can be enabled with the following command: $ sudo systemctl enable auditd.service
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q audit; }; then SYSTEMCTL_EXEC='/usr/bin/systemctl' "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" unmask 'auditd.service' if [[ $("$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" is-system-running) != "offline" ]]; then "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" start 'auditd.service' fi "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" enable 'auditd.service' else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Enabling the auditd service is a standard STIG remediation that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical boot components. The provided script simply unmasks, starts, and enables the service, which is low risk and reversible.

Use `systemctl enable --now auditd.service` to combine enabling and starting in one atomic operation.
After enabling, verify the service state with `systemctl is-active auditd.service` and log the result.

Slight CPU overhead from audit processing

vulnerability.title: System Audit Logs Must Have Mode 0640 or Less Permissive
vulnerability.description: Determine where the audit logs are stored with the following command: $ sudo grep -iw log_file /etc/audit/auditd.conf log_file = /var/log/audit/audit.log Configure the audit log to be protected from unauthorized read access by setting the correct permissive mode with the following command: $ sudo chmod 0600 audit_log_file By default, audit_log_file is "/var/log/audit/audit.log".
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q audit && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then if LC_ALL=C grep -iw ^log_file /etc/audit/auditd.conf; then FILE=$(awk -F "=" '/^log_file/ {print $2}' /etc/audit/auditd.conf | tr -d ' ') else FILE="/var/log/audit/audit.log" fi chmod 0600 $FILE else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: The remediation only changes the mode of the audit log file to a more restrictive setting (0600). This does not affect system boot, SSH, or critical services and follows standard STIG hardening for file permissions.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential temporary loss of read access to audit logs for non‑privileged users

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly identifies the log file path and applies the required 0600 permissions. It will remediate the vulnerability as described.
concerns: The script does not handle the case where the audit service is running and may have the file open, which could cause the chmod to be ignored until a log rotation or service restart.

qa_result.verdict_reason: Setting the audit log file to mode 0600 reduces permissions to root only, which is the recommended hardening. auditd runs as root and can still write to the file, and no other critical services depend on group read access. The script checks for file existence before chmod, avoiding errors on missing files.
side_effects: If the audit log file is currently open, chmod may not take effect until the file is rotated or auditd is restarted, but this does not cause a service outage.
Future log rotations will create new files with default permissions; the fix must be reapplied or a umask configured accordingly.
